title: Trouble with rootless podman over ansible or distcc
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

I use rootless podman underneath distcc to serve various compiler images. If I let distcc or ansible execute "podman pull", under the distcc user, then I get the dreaded "there might not be enough IDs" message. If I execute the command from a login shell, then it works. I suspect that podman is misdetecting its own configuration if not running under a login shell.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1.  ansible --become-user=distcc -some_host -m podman_image -a name=

(same problem happens with "ansible -a" or if distcc itself executes it as part of "podman run")


**Describe the results you received:**

There might not be enough IDs available.

**Describe the results you expected:**

Pull complete.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

podman version 1.8.0

```
(paste your output here)
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
(paste your output here)
```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

Physical, Fedora 30

comments.body: It's already known and documented that Podman does not play nicely with `sudo` and `su` (they don't play nicely with some PAM things that we expect, for example mounting `/run/user/$UID`), so I suspect that this may be similar; however, I've never seen an ID-related error involved in that. I don't know exactly how `become_user` works, but it's possible that it's doing things in a strange manner that is causing us to not properly detect what user we're running as.

That's unfortunate. What do people do? 

Is there a list of such requirements that I can try to satisfy myself?
The failure could be much more graceful. If /run/blah doesn't exist, tell me that's the problem, don't tell me that there might not be enough IDs available.

I worked around this by giving this command to ansible: `sudo sudo -i -u distcc podman pull ...`. Of course ansible cursed me for using sudo.

Please make it easier to work with rootless podman in non-interactive scenarios. And also try to improve error reporting, the "not enough IDs" message is thrown whenever there is some misconfiguration, but there is no clue as to what the misconfiguration is.

If sudo actually works, it sounds like something about Ansible's become is
confusing the rootless code majorly. I wouldn't consider this a
misconfiguration, but rather Podman likely not being entirely sure what
user it was run as and not locating the proper subuid entry.

On Sun, May 3, 2020, 07:50 Avi Kivity <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> I worked around this by giving this command to ansible: sudo sudo -i -u
> distcc podman pull .... Of course ansible cursed me for using sudo.
>
> Please make it easier to work with rootless podman in non-interactive
> scenarios. And also try to improve error reporting, the "not enough IDs"
> message is thrown whenever there is some misconfiguration, but there is no
> clue as to what the misconfiguration is.
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you commented.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/containers/libpod/issues/6068#issuecomment-623097872>,
> or unsubscribe
> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/AB3AOCDRVDZOZ4XUPC535ETRPVLB3ANCNFSM4MXYUTIQ>
> .
>

It works after I added the service that kept /run/user/$(id foo) up (I did not do exhaustive checks to see if the exact incantation that ended up working does not work without it).

Is it possible to make podman's notion of the user more consistent with other system tools? And perhaps report the user it thinks it is when consulting subuid?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

@giuseppe @mheon Could you guys add something to troubleshooting on this.

Basically document
````
ssh root@HOSTNAME
# su user
$ podman ...
Failure
```

@rhatdan, I am not sure what to document.

`su --login` was reported to work. I believe we need to further investigation on what works and what not.

The basic problem is the lack of XDG_RUNTIME_DIR being set or other fields set incorrectly so that podman tries to do stuff that the root process can only do.  IE Write the /root homedir or /run/user/0/...

We have to document that the user needs to setup a true user login session by either coming in through a login process or by executine `su -- USER`.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
This is now documented, closing. Reopen if I am mistaken.
